Built for the Code That Actually Governs California Projects

California doesn't run on IBC.

ArchiMind was built from the ground up for this stack: [...] aec+tech
Advanced Search

ArchiMind

# ArchiMind

Website for ArchiMind

ARCHIMIND is a native Autodesk Revit add-in that runs California Building Code (CBC 2025 & 2022) compliance checks directly inside Revit. It automates 182+ code checks across six chapters — including egress, accessibility, plumbing, height and area, fire resistance, and light and ventilation — and delivers results in under 60 seconds. Designed for architecture firms, ARCHIMIND eliminates manual code lookups, reduces permit rejection risk, and integrates seamlessly into the existing Revit workflow without switching tools or exporting files.

Building Information Modeling (BIM)

Artificial Intelligence (AI)

Architectural Design

Building Performance

Key Facts

Similar Tools

FAQ

Comments

## Gallery [...] Occupancy Load · Egress · Stairs & Ramps · Fire Resistance · Height & Area · Light & Ventilation · Chapter 11B Accessibility · Restroom Fixtures · Plumbing Fixture Count · EVCS Parking · Exit Signs · DSA Path of Travel

Every result is tied to a specific Revit element and cited to the exact code section. Not "something might be wrong." Exactly what, exactly where, exactly why.

Not Just Flagging — Fixing

Most compliance tools stop at flagging. ArchiMind goes further.

Click GoTo on any failing check — Revit zooms directly to the element. Click Fix — ArchiMind corrects it automatically. Door too narrow? Fixed. Handrail too low? Fixed. Ramp too steep? Fixed. Stair too narrow? Fixed. Corridor too tight? Fixed. Every fix is fully undoable with Ctrl+Z. Re-run the analysis to confirm.

results.title: GitHub Repository Analysis with Full-Context AI
results.content:
# GitHub Repository Analysis with Full-Context AI

RepoMind helps teams evaluate unfamiliar repositories by mapping architecture, tracing implementation behavior, and surfacing risk hotspots that affect delivery confidence.

The workflow is designed for practical decisions: whether to adopt a dependency, how to onboard quickly, and where to focus review effort when timelines are tight.

## What this workflow delivers

RepoMind does more than summarize files. It highlights how modules connect, where critical logic resides, and which areas deserve deeper review before engineering investment increases.

### Architecture clarity for faster onboarding

Teams can understand major boundaries and dependencies early, reducing time spent in manual code discovery. [...] ## Frequently Asked Questions

### What is GitHub repository analysis in RepoMind?

It is a workflow that maps architecture, behavior paths, and risk hotspots so teams can make faster technical decisions.

### When should teams run repository analysis?

Run it during onboarding, due diligence, migration planning, security triage preparation, and major release checkpoints.

### Can this be used for open-source adoption decisions?

Yes. Teams use it to evaluate maintainability, architecture complexity, and likely remediation effort before adoption.

### Who benefits the most from this workflow?

Platform teams, senior engineers, technical leads, and security reviewers who need fast context on unfamiliar codebases.

### Does it replace code review or security scanning? [...] ### Does it replace code review or security scanning?

No. It complements both by establishing a shared architecture context before deeper review and security workflows.

### What should happen after the first analysis run?

Use identified hotspots to prioritize review and security actions, then track improvements through recurring analysis checkpoints.

## Take the next step

Analyze one critical repository and convert context into a prioritized review and security plan.

Analyze a GitHub RepositoryOpen AI Code Review ToolBack to Solutions

results.title: How to Analyze Any GitHub Repository Like a Security Engineer
results.content:
Start by asking: What problem does this repository solve? Who is it designed for, and what is the primary use case? A repository that claims to be a “lightweight log forwarder” but ships with a full web dashboard and SQL database is already telling you something important about scope and complexity.

Next, map the full technology stack. List every language, framework, runtime, and major dependency. Ask yourself why each was chosen over alternatives. A Go-based network tool that also imports a heavy Python ML library for “anomaly detection” deserves deeper scrutiny into how those two runtimes communicate and where the boundary lies. [...] With the foundation established, move to architecture analysis. This is where you build a mental model of how the system actually works — not just what it claims to do.

Request or construct a bird’s-eye view of the major components: services, modules, layers, and how they interconnect. Look for the threading and concurrency model. Is this system synchronous or event-driven? Does it use async I/O or multi-threading? These decisions have direct implications for race conditions, deadlocks, and data integrity under load. [...] ## Conclusion

A GitHub repository is not just code. It is a system with assumptions, dependencies, trust relationships, and an attack surface — all of which deserve careful examination before you deploy it anywhere that matters.

The framework above gives you a systematic, repeatable process for developing that understanding. It is organized from the outside in: start with what the system is and who it serves, understand how it works architecturally, follow the data through every transformation, audit the security posture methodically, assess infrastructure exposure, and finally verify that it is operationally ready for production.

#AI #GitHub #NextJS #TypeScript #OpenSource #LLM #SoftwareEngineering #WebDevelopment #DeveloperTools #Programming [...] # I Built an AI That Understands GitHub Repositories | Arch Logs #002
## wildtrance
14 subscribers
5 likes

### Description
150 views
Posted: 28 Jun 2026
RepoMind is an AI-powered GitHub repository intelligence platform that helps developers understand unfamiliar codebases faster through natural language.

Instead of manually navigating hundreds of files, RepoMind analyzes repository structure, builds contextual understanding, and provides insights into architecture, dependencies, implementation, security, and overall project design.

In this video, I walk through the motivation behind the project, the engineering decisions that shaped it, the system architecture, and the challenges of building an AI capable of understanding real-world repositories.

## Features [...] Building a mental map of the massive unfamiliar code base takes days of reading messy documents and tracing imports. So, I built RepoMind, a self-hosting multi-agent AI engine that clones, parses, and visually maps out entire software repositories natively in production. You can audit the full code base on my GitHub and try out the live web application right now. RepoMind operates via a strict asynchronous three-tiered ingestion pipeline: clone, parse, and embed. When you drop a URL, an Express background worker picks it up. We parse the URL with Octokit and invoke simple get to clone the target repository directly into the temporary local buffer. Instead of using unreliable regex machining like standard AI wrappers, the system passes the files to TS Morph, a wrapper over the native
